Formulary Body: CADTH (Canadian Drug and Health Technology Agency)
Pricing Mechanism: PMPRB caps patented drug prices using international reference pricing. Provincial formularies negotiate reimbursement prices separately. Generic prices set at percentage of brand price.
Generic Price Rule: First generic: 25% of brand price in most provinces. Additional generics drive further competition.
International Reference Basket: France, Germany, Italy, Sweden, Switzerland, UK, USA — PMPRB compares to median
Price Transparency: Moderate — ODB formulary prices public, confidential rebates not disclosed
Prescription Charge to Patient: Varies by province and coverage. ODB: $2 copay for eligible. No coverage: full cost.
Key Strength: PMPRB caps prevent extreme pricing. Provincial bulk purchasing power.
Key Weakness: Fragmented provincial system. Uninsured Canadians pay full price. Slower formulary listing than UK.

Pricing Authority: None — no federal drug price regulation for most drugs
Formulary Body: PBMs (Pharmacy Benefit Managers) negotiate on behalf of insurers
Pricing Mechanism: Manufacturers set list price freely. PBMs negotiate rebates. Net price often much lower than list price but savings not passed to uninsured patients. Medicare Part D negotiation introduced 2022 IRA for limited drugs.
Generic Price Rule: Hatch-Waxman Act enables generic entry after patent expiry. First generic gets 180-day exclusivity then open competition.
International Reference Basket: None — USA does not reference international prices
Price Transparency: Low — list price public but net price after rebates confidential
Avg Generic vs Brand Price Ratio: 80 to 90% initially, drops to 20% with multiple generics
Prescription Charge to Patient: Highly variable. Uninsured: full list price. Insured: copay varies by plan.
Key Strength: Fastest approval timelines globally. Strong generic competition eventually drives prices down.
Key Weakness: No price negotiation for most drugs. Highest brand prices globally. 25M+ uninsured Americans pay list price.

Regulatory Body: MHRA (Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency)
Pricing Authority: DHSC (Department of Health and Social Care) — VPAS scheme
Formulary Body: NICE (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence)
Pricing Mechanism: NICE assesses cost-effectiveness using QALY threshold of £20,000 to £30,000 per QALY. Drugs not meeting threshold not recommended on NHS unless manufacturer negotiates price down. VPAS scheme caps industry-wide NHS medicines spending growth.
Generic Price Rule: Drug Tariff sets reimbursement price for generics. Prices updated monthly based on market conditions.
International Reference Basket: NICE uses cost-effectiveness analysis rather than international reference pricing
Price Transparency: High — NICE appraisals published publicly. Drug Tariff prices public monthly.
Avg Generic vs Brand Price Ratio: 10 to 30% — NHS drives aggressive generic substitution
Prescription Charge to Patient: Flat £9.90 per item regardless of drug cost (May 2024). Free for under 16, over 60, and certain conditions.
Key Strength: NICE QALY threshold creates price discipline. Flat prescription charge protects patients. High price transparency.
Key Weakness: Slower access — NICE appraisal adds time after MHRA approval. Some drugs never recommended due to cost.

Pricing Mechanism: NPPA sets ceiling prices for all drugs on the NLEM using market-based pricing of existing products. Non-NLEM drugs can be priced freely by manufacturers. Annual price increase capped at WPI inflation rate.
Generic Price Rule: Jan Aushadhi scheme provides generic drugs at 50 to 90% below brand price through government stores.
International Reference Basket: India does not use international reference pricing — uses domestic market average
Price Transparency: High for NLEM drugs — ceiling prices publicly listed on NPPA website
Prescription Charge to Patient: Varies widely. Government hospitals: free or nominal. Private: full price. Jan Aushadhi: heavily subsidised.
Key Strength: Lowest prices globally for essential medicines. World's pharmacy — supplies generics to 200+ countries.
Key Weakness: Quality control variable outside regulated manufacturers. Access in rural areas poor. Non-essential drugs priced freely.
